{{Short description|Process of constraining a physical quantity to discrete values}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Quantization (physics)
| image = 
| caption = 
| topics = [[Quantum mechanics]], [[Energy]], [[Wave–particle duality]]
}}

In [[physics]], '''quantization''' is the process of transition from a classical understanding of physical phenomena to a newer understanding known as [[quantum mechanics]]. It is the procedure for constructing a quantum theory from a classical theory. More generally, quantization refers to the fact that certain physical quantities can take only certain discrete values rather than a continuous spectrum.

The concept dates to [[Max Planck]]'s 1900 resolution of the [[ultraviolet catastrophe]] in [[black-body radiation]], where he proposed that [[electromagnetic radiation|electromagnetic energy]] is emitted and absorbed in discrete quanta of energy <math>E = h\nu</math>.

== Canonical quantization ==
In the canonical approach, classical [[Poisson bracket]]s are replaced by [[commutator]]s:
:<math>\{A, B\} \rightarrow \frac{1}{i\hbar}[\hat{A}, \hat{B}]</math>

For position and momentum, this leads to the fundamental commutation relation:
:<math>[\hat{x}, \hat{p}] = i\hbar</math>

This commutation relation directly implies the [[Heisenberg uncertainty principle]]:
:<math>\Delta x \, \Delta p \geq \frac{\hbar}{2}</math>

== Path integral quantization ==
Developed by [[Richard Feynman]], the [[path integral formulation]] expresses the quantum amplitude as a sum over all possible classical paths:
:<math>\langle x_f | e^{-iHt/\hbar} | x_i \rangle = \int \mathcal{D}x(t) \, e^{iS[x]/\hbar}</math>

== Examples of quantized systems ==

=== Energy quantization ===
* [[Particle in a box]]: <math>E_n = \frac{n^2\pi^2\hbar^2}{2mL^2}</math>
* [[Quantum harmonic oscillator]]: <math>E_n = \hbar\omega(n + \frac{1}{2})</math>
* [[Hydrogen atom]]: <math>E_n = -\frac{13.6\ \text{eV}}{n^2}</math>

=== Angular momentum quantization ===
The magnitude and one component of [[angular momentum]] are quantized:
:<math>L^2 = \ell(\ell+1)\hbar^2</math>
:<math>L_z = m_\ell\hbar</math>

=== Charge quantization ===
[[Electric charge]] appears only in integer multiples of the [[elementary charge]] <math>e</math>.

== Second quantization ==
In [[quantum field theory]], not only physical quantities but also the fields themselves are promoted to operators, allowing the description of particle creation and annihilation.

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Energy level]]
* [[Quantum mechanics]]
* [[Quantum number]]
* [[Uncertainty principle]]
* [[Wave function]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Quantum mechanics]]
[[Category:Mathematical quantization]]
